epigraph.text: The steadfast love of the Lord never ceases; his mercies never come to an end.
epigraph.quote: When does the path not matter? When there is an antiderivative.

content.text:
In real calculus, the Fundamental Theorem tells us that integrals of 
derivatives depend only on endpoints. The same principle holds in complex 
analysis, with profound consequences.

content.title: Antiderivative
content.content:
Let $f$ be continuous on a domain $D$. A function $F$ analytic on $D$ is 
an **antiderivative** of $f$ if $F'(z) = f(z)$ 
for all $z \in D$.

content.title: Fundamental Theorem of Contour Integration
content.content:
Let $f$ be continuous on a domain $D$ and suppose $f$ has an antiderivative 
$F$ on $D$. Then for any contour $\gamma$ in $D$ from $z_1$ to $z_2$:
\[
 \int_\gamma f(z)\,dz = F(z_2) - F(z_1).
\]
In particular, the integral depends only on the endpoints, not on the path.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Parametrize $\gamma: [a, b] \to \C$ with $\gamma(a) = z_1$, $\gamma(b) = z_2$.
$$
 \int_\gamma f(z)\,dz &= \int_a^b f(\gamma(t))\gamma'(t)\,dt 
 = \int_a^b F'(\gamma(t))\gamma'(t)\,dt \\
 &= \int_a^b \frac{d}{dt}[F(\gamma(t))]\,dt = F(\gamma(b)) - F(\gamma(a)) 
 = F(z_2) - F(z_1).
$$

content.problem: Compute $\displaystyle\int_\gamma z^2\,dz$ from $0$ to $1 + i$ along any path.

content.title: Closed Contour Integral
content.content:
If $f$ has an antiderivative on a domain $D$ and $\gamma$ is a closed 
contour in $D$, then
\[
 \oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 0.
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $\gamma$ starts and ends at $z_0$, then 
$\int_\gamma f\,dz = F(z_0) - F(z_0) = 0$.

content.title: When antiderivatives don't exist
content.content:
The function $f(z) = 1/z$ has no antiderivative on any domain containing 
a loop around the origin. This is why $\oint_{|z|=1} \frac{dz}{z} = 2\pi i \neq 0$.

content.title: Antiderivative Existence
content.content:
Let $f$ be analytic on a domain $D$. If 
$\displaystyle\oint_\gamma f(z)\,dz = 0$ for every closed contour 
$\gamma$ in $D$, then $f$ has an antiderivative on $D$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The proof constructs $F(z) = \int_{z_0}^z f(\zeta)\,d\zeta$ along any 
path from a fixed $z_0$ to $z$; the condition ensures this is well-defined.

marginNotes.text:
Come to me, all who labor and are 
heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn 
from me.

marginNotes.author: Isaac Newton
marginNotes.text:
If I have seen further, it is by standing on 
the shoulders of giants.
